questions.promptTex: \text{The four angles of any quadrilateral sum to:}
questions.explanation: $(n - 2) \cdot 180° = (4 - 2) \cdot 180 = 360°$. Or: split the quadrilateral into two triangles with a diagonal — each triangle gives 180°.

hints: All rhombuses have 2 pairs of parallel sides.
What's a quadrilateral with 2 pairs of parallel sides?
questions.explanation: A rhombus has 2 pairs of parallel sides → it's always a **parallelogram**. (Not always a rectangle — only if its angles are 90°.)

questions.promptTex: \text{A parallelogram has one angle of } 50°. \text{ The angle adjacent to it (sharing a side) is:}
hints: Consecutive angles in a parallelogram are supplementary.
questions.explanation: Consecutive angles of a parallelogram are supplementary: $180° - 50° = 130°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which property is unique to rectangles (among parallelograms)?}
hints: All parallelograms have the first, second, and fourth properties.
What's special about rectangle diagonals?
questions.explanation: Diagonals bisect each other in *every* parallelogram. But diagonals are **equal in length** only when the parallelogram is a rectangle (or square).

choices: A rectangle but not a rhombus
A rhombus but not a rectangle
Both a rectangle AND a rhombus
hints: A square has 4 right angles AND 4 equal sides.
Right angles → rectangle. Equal sides → rhombus.
questions.explanation: A square has 4 right angles (so it's a rectangle) AND 4 equal sides (so it's a rhombus). The intersection of the two — that's exactly what a square is.

questions.promptTex: \text{A quadrilateral with all four sides equal AND four right angles is a:}
hints: All sides equal → at least a rhombus. All angles right → at least a rectangle.
Both conditions → square.
questions.explanation: All sides equal + all right angles = square.
steps: All four sides equal: classifies as a rhombus.
All four right angles: classifies as a rectangle.
Both conditions together: **square** (the most specific quadrilateral, intersection of rhombus and rectangle).

questions.promptTex: \text{A rectangle has length } 12 \text{ and width } 5. \text{ Find its diagonal.}
hints: Diagonal = $\sqrt{L^2 + W^2}$.
steps: A rectangle's diagonal is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $L$ and $W$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A parallelogram has consecutive angles } x° \text{ and } (3x + 20)°. \\ \text{Find } x.
hints: Consecutive angles in a parallelogram are SUPPLEMENTARY.
steps: Consecutive angles of a parallelogram are supplementary.
$$4x + 20 = 180 \Rightarrow 4x = 160 \Rightarrow x = 40$$

questions.promptTex: \text{A rhombus has diagonals of length } 6 \text{ and } 8. \\ \text{Find the length of one side.}
hints: Diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at right angles.
Each side is the hypotenuse of a triangle with legs 3 and 4.
steps: The diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other and are perpendicular.
So each side is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $6/2 = 3$ and $8/2 = 4$.

questions.promptTex: \text{An isosceles trapezoid has base angles of } 70°. \\ \text{What is the measure of each top angle?}
hints: Base + top angle on the same leg are supplementary.
steps: In a trapezoid, angles on the same leg (bottom and top of one side) are supplementary because the bases are parallel.
(In an isosceles trapezoid, both top angles are $110°$ and both base angles are $70°$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is true of the diagonals of a rectangle (but NOT a parallelogram in general)?}
hints: All parallelograms have diagonals that bisect each other.
Only rectangles (and squares) have equal-length diagonals.
questions.explanation: Diagonals of any parallelogram bisect each other. But only rectangles (and squares) have **equal-length** diagonals.
steps: Bisecting: true for all parallelograms.
Perpendicular: true only for rhombi (and squares).
**Equal length:** true for rectangles (and squares) — special property.
Bisecting angles: true only for rhombi.
**Answer:** equal in length.

questions.promptTex: \text{A rhombus has side length } 13 \text{ and one diagonal } 10. \\ \text{Find the other diagonal.}
hints: The diagonals are perpendicular bisectors. Half-diagonals are legs of a right triangle with side as hypotenuse.
$\sqrt{13^2 - 5^2} = 12$ is half the other diagonal.
questions.explanation: Half of one diagonal: $5$. Other half-diagonal $= \sqrt{13^2 - 5^2} = 12$. Other diagonal $= 2 \cdot 12 = 24$.
steps: In a rhombus, diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
Half of the known diagonal: $10/2 = 5$.
Each side ($13$) is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs = half-diagonals.
Other half-diagonal: $\sqrt{13^2 - 5^2} = \sqrt{169 - 25} = \sqrt{144} = 12$.
Full other diagonal: $2 \cdot 12 = 24$. (5-12-13 triple.)
